title: SEGMENT 13 — Jennifer Lewis and sacrificial printing: making channels by removing them

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Jennifer Lewis and sacrificial printing: making channels by removing them
The most influential recent advance in vascular bioprinting came out of Jennifer Lewis's lab, first at the University of Illinois and then at Harvard.
The Lewis lab spent years developing a beautiful technique called sacrificial printing, and the underlying idea is so clever that once you hear it, you wonder why no one had done it earlier.
Here is the trick.
Suppose you want to build a tissue construct with internal channels in the shape of blood vessels — branching, tapering, with capillary-scale terminals.
Directly printing hollow channels is hard.
You'd need to extrude a tube of material that doesn't collapse, with walls that are biocompatible, at the right diameter — and you'd need to keep the inside hollow while the outside cures.
That's mechanically tricky and limits what shapes you can achieve.
What Lewis's group realized is that you don't have to print the channels directly.

You can print the negative of the channels — print solid filaments in the shape you want the channels to take, embedded inside the tissue construct, and then remove those filaments after the construct is cured, leaving behind hollow channels.
The filaments are sacrificial.
They serve as a template that gets dissolved away.
It's the same idea as the lost-wax casting technique sculptors have used for millennia, transplanted into hydrogel chemistry.
The specific material Lewis's group settled on was a substance called Pluronic F-127, a synthetic block copolymer with a temperature-dependent gel property.
At body temperature, Pluronic is a stiff gel and prints well.
At cold temperatures — refrigerator cold, around 4 degrees Celsius — Pluronic becomes a liquid and flows.
So the workflow is this.
Print a network of Pluronic filaments, in the shape of the vascular tree you want, on a substrate.
Cast a hydrogel — typically GelMA with cells embedded — around and over the Pluronic filaments, encapsulating them in the tissue construct.
Cure the GelMA with UV light, locking the tissue structure in place.
Cool the whole thing down.
The Pluronic liquefies.
Flow water or medium through the channels to wash the Pluronic out.
What you're left with is a tissue construct with a network of hollow channels exactly where the Pluronic filaments had been.
Line those channels with endothelial cells, which colonize the channel walls and form an endothelium.
Perfuse the network with medium.
You now have a vascularized tissue construct.
The analogy is this.
Sacrificial printing is a build-then-remove trick.
Print blood-vessel-shaped Pluronic gel inside your tissue.
Cast hydrogel around it.
Flow water through to dissolve the Pluronic.
You have a vascular channel ready to be lined with endothelial cells.
The Lewis group has used this technique to build remarkably complex vascularized tissue constructs.

Their most famous demonstrations include thick cardiac tissue constructs with branching vascular networks, vascularized kidney organoid assemblies, and proximal tubule constructs with parallel channel networks mimicking nephron architecture.
Recent work has pushed this to extremely high vessel densities — networks of channels with capillary-scale spacing in centimeter-scale constructs.

This isn't the full solution — these are still benchtop demonstrations, not transplantable organs, and the channels aren't perfectly capillary-scale yet — but it's the most concrete progress on the vascularization problem in many years.
Sacrificial printing, in various forms, has become a standard tool in modern bioprinting.
Other labs have developed variants using different sacrificial materials — carbohydrate glasses, sacrificial sugar-based filaments, even printed gelatin filaments that melt away at body temperature.
The general principle of "print the negative, then remove it" is now the dominant strategy for building internal vasculature into engineered tissues.

If you read about the field in the coming years and you see breathless announcements about "vascularized printed organs," there's a good chance the underlying technique traces back to Lewis-style sacrificial printing.
The technique is real.
The progress is real.
The path from these constructs to clinically usable thick tissues is, however, still long.
